STA237A Hw1

Eric Kalosa-Kenyon

28 September 2017

Abstract

In this assignment, I solve basic problems in time series.
  • Stationarity
  • US Population
    • Plot the data
    • Fit a polynomial
    • Model diagnostics
    • Predict 2010 and 2020
  • Sources

Stationarity

In this section, I calculate the mean and autocovariance functions (ACVF) of each of the following time series. Additionally, I specify which time series are weakly and strongly stationary. Note that the random variables \(Z\) are independent normal with mean \(0\) and variance \(\sigma^2\); \(a,b,\textrm{and } c\) are constants.

  1. \(X_t = a + bZ_t + cZ_{t-1}\)
  1. This process is strictly stationary. To see this, consider that the \(Z\) random variables are independent, so \(X_t\) is distributed according to \(N(a, \sigma^2(b^2+c^2))\). Hence, \(\{X_t\}\) is a Gaussian time series. A multivariate Gaussian is fully characterized by its first two moments[1], which are here known. It is also weakly stationary because the second moment is finite.
  2. \(E(X_t) = E(a + bZ_t + cZ_{t-1}) = a\)
  3. \(Cov(X_t, X_{t-1}) = E((X_t-E(X_t))(X_{t-1}-E(X_{t-1}))) = E(X_t X_{t-1}) - a^2 = bcE(Z_{t-1}^2) = bc(\sigma^2+a^2)\). The function is \(0\) when considering the autocovariance of lags deeper than \(\Delta t=1\).
  1. \(X_t = Z_t\cos(ct) + Z_{t-1}\sin(ct)\)
  1. This time series is not stationary. Consider the autocorrelation with lag \(\Delta t=1\): \(Cov(X_t,X_{t-1}) = E((Z_tcos(ct)+Z_{t-1}sin(ct))(Z_{t-1}cos(ct)+Z_{t-2}sin(ct))) = E(Z_{t-1}^2sin(ct)cos(ct)) = sin(ct)cos(ct)\sigma^2\). This autocorrelation function depends on \(t\) so the corresponding time series is therefore not stationary.
  1. \(X_t = a + bZ_0\)
  1. This time series is clearly strictly and weakly stationary: each \(X_t\) is a Gaussian random variable with mean \(a\) and variance \(b^2\sigma^2\). The mean and variance of the random variables are also the mean and ACVF of the time series.
  1. \(X_t = Z_tZ_{t-1}\)
  1. The ACVF of this time series is \(0\) everywhere except when there is a lag of \(\Delta t = 1\) when it is \(\gamma(1)=\sigma^2\). This doesn’t depend on \(t\) so the time series is weakly stationary. Its mean is \(0\). It is also strictly stationary because the joint distribution doesn’t depend on \(t\).

US Population

In this section, I perform a basic analysis of the US population from 1790 to 2000.

Plot the data

In this section, I perform a first pass visualization of the data.

library("gdata") # read.xls inside

df = read.xls("population.xls", header=FALSE)
names(df) = c("Population")
df$Date = seq(from=1790, to=2000, by=10)

plot(df$Date, df$Population, main="Population from 1790 to 2000",
    xlab="Date", ylab="Population")

Fit a polynomial

In this section, I fit a polynomial of degree 2 to the census data.

m = lm(Population~poly(Date, 2, raw=TRUE), data=df)
names(m$coefficients)[2:3] = c("x1", "x2")

plt.df = data.frame(x=df$Date, y=predict(m))
plot(plt.df, type="l", main="Polynomial fit to trend",
     xlab="Date", ylab="Population")
points(df$Date, df$Population)

Model diagnostics

In this section, I perform a brief analysis of the model diagnostics for the polynomial census model.

par(mfrow=c(2,2))
plot(m)

The residuals are not heteroskedastic, but they’re not terrible either. There are certianly a few outliers, namely points 16, 17, and 22. A more sophisticated model might be appropriate, but I suspect this simple model will perform well in near term predictions.

Predict 2010 and 2020

In this section, I use the polynomial model to predict the US population in the years 2010 and 2020.

yrs = data.frame(Date=c(2010, 2020))
future_pop = predict(m, yrs)

The US population in 2010 may be \(3.0435131\times 10^{8}\), and the population in 2020 may be \(3.3327299\times 10^{8}\).
